retrieved_docs.title: Case Study: 60-Year-Old Female Presenting With Shortness of Breath (Archived) -- Case Presentation
retrieved_docs.content: She reports difficulty breathing at rest, forgetfulness, mild fatigue, feeling chilled, requiring blankets, increased urinary frequency, incontinence, and swelling in her bilateral lower extremities that are new-onset and worsening. Subsequently, she has not ambulated from bed for several days except to use the restroom due to feeling weak, fatigued, and short of breath.

retrieved_docs.title: Danon Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, males show signs at a younger age, including muscle weakness and possibly delayed motor skills. Larger muscle groups are typically affected, including the back, shoulder, neck, and upper legs. [1] This condition may manifest as difficulty with arm raise, getting up from a chair, or walking up a flight of stairs. Fatigue, dyspnea, and lower extremity edema may be a manifestation of progressive cardiomyopathy. Males more often present with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (90%), while females may manifest equally with either hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or dilated cardiomyopathy. Sudden cardiac death from cardiac disease occurs more frequently in males in their second and third decades of life. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Inclusion Body Myositis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The initial presenting symptom can vary from patient to patient. The most common feature is an insidious onset and progressive course of muscle weakness which manifests as the following: Difficulty in climbing stairs or arising from a chair (pelvic girdle) Decrease in walking speed (hip flexors) Frequent falls due to buckling of the knees (quadriceps) Foot drop and frequent tripping (ankle dorsiflexion) Difficulty in combing hair and in reaching overhead cabinets (shoulder girdle) Decrease in grip strength (finger flexors) Neck muscle involvement can result in difficulty in lifting the head from a pillow. The distinguishing features of IBM from other forms of inflammatory myopathies are the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Myopathy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Myopathies are typically involving motor impairment without no sensory symptoms. It presents as proximal muscle weakness, mainly in the pelvic girdle or the shoulder girdle muscle groups. However, pelvic muscle group is more common and more severe. Comprehensive history alongside physical examination is mandatory to identify and diagnose myopathies. Patients may complain of difficulty raising up from sitting position, climbing stairs or difficulty brushing their hair, or practicing any above head activities. Some other myopathies will present in different muscle groups like thighs, back muscles, or fingers, and could be possibly associated with other symptoms like myalgia, rashes, fatigue, or cramps. Muscle disease could also present with dark urine as a sign of renal damage in the case of rhabdomyolysis, particularly after vigorous exercises like running marathons.

retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms Decline in brain concentration on tasks Erectile dysfunction and decreased libido Hot flashes Muscle weakness and loss of muscle mass Depression and irritability Pain and tenderness in the testis Infertility [8] Physical examination findings Small and firm testes Gynecomastia Absence or reduction in sperm production Decreased body and facial hair [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Dyspnea on Exertion(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for dyspnea on exertion depends on its underlying etiology. The first intervention is to determine that there are no life-threatening etiologies present on an acute presentation by monitoring the ABCs (airway, breathing, and circulation) of the patient. Once it is determined to be stable and that no immediate lifesaving interventions are necessary, an assessment for further treatment can be made. If a patient is a tobacco smoker, this should be discontinued. Various inhaler therapies may be used in respiratory disease, including short-acting or long-acting bronchodilators, inhaled antimuscarinics, and inhaled corticosteroids. Continuous supplemental oxygen therapy is used to ease discomfort associated with dyspnea on exertion if oxygen saturation is shown to decrease with exercise. [8] Cardiac function should be optimized when a cardiac illness is identified. If myocardial infarction is suspected based on ST changes on the electrocardiogram or troponin marker evaluation, rapid percutaneous intervention should be performed by a cardiologist. Therapy with aspirin, statin, ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, heparin, and nitrates should be initiated immediately if no contraindications exist. Occasionally, medications such as beta-blockers and calcium-channel blockers can induce dyspnea on exertion by decreasing cardiac function, which can be picked up on a CPET. These should be decreased or discontinued when possible. In CHF, diuretic medications should be used to decrease vascular congestion from fluid overloading. If the dyspnea on exertion is due to obesity or deconditioning, a physical therapy exercise regimen should be pursued. If psychological problems are causing dyspnea on exertion, a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor can be tried along with counseling sessions. [9] Weight loss in obese patients, especially women, will improve outcomes. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Male Urinary Retention: Acute and Chronic -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of renal failure may be the first clue of chronic urinary retention. These symptoms include: Decreased urine output, although occasionally urine output remains normal Confusion Edema with swelling of the legs, ankles, and feet Fatigue Fluid retention Irregular heartbeat Nausea Shortness of breath Weakness

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Outflow Tract Obstruction -- Issues of Concern -- Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms may include chest pain, dyspnea, exertional fatigue, dizziness, palpitation, and other symptoms of heart failure. Patients may experience near-syncope or syncope due to outflow obstruction or arrhythmia. On precordial examination, a harsh crescendo-decrescendo murmur is heard at the apex. This murmur varies in intensity with different physiological states. It increases in intensity with Valsalva or nitrates and decreases with squatting or hand grip.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Thyroid Hormone -- Clinical Significance -- Symptoms of Hypothyroidism
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with hypothyroidism may present with myxedema caused by decreased clearance of complex glycosaminoglycans and hyaluronic acids from the reticular layer of the dermis. Initially, the nonpitting edema is pretibial. As the state of hypothyroidism continues, patients can develop generalized edema. Symptoms related to decreased metabolic rate: Bradycardia Fatigue Cold intolerance Weight gain Poor appetite Hair loss Cold and dry skin Constipation Myopathy, stiffness, cramps, entrapment syndromes Delayed deep tendon reflex relaxation Symptoms from generalized myxedema: Myxedematous heart disease Puffy appearance with doughy skin texture Hoarse voice with difficulty articulating words Pretibial and periorbital edema Symptoms of hyperprolactinemia: Amenorrhea or menorrhagia Galactorrhea Erectile dysfunction, infertility in men Decreased libido Other symptoms: Depression Impaired concentration and memory Goiter Hypertension Congenital hypothyroidism:

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothyroid Myopathy -- Staging -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Severe: Severe hypothyroid myopathy is characterized by profound muscle weakness and fatigue, significantly impairing mobility and functional independence. Patients may struggle to perform basic activities of daily living, such as dressing, bathing, or climbing stairs, and may require assistance or mobility aids.

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Arterial Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with severe PAD can develop ischemic rest pain. These patients do not walk enough to claudicate because of their severe disease. Instead, they complain of burning pain in the soles of their feet that is worse at night. They cannot sleep due to the pain and often dangle their lower leg over the side of the bed in an attempt to relieve their discomfort. The slight increase in blood flow due to gravity temporarily diminishes the otherwise intractable pain. In some cases, edema from keeping the leg in a dependent position may be mistakenly attributed to venous thrombosis. Other features of PAD include erectile dysfunction, which is often an early indicator of the disease. The physical exam may reveal the following: Loss of pulses Pain on palpation Pallor Muscle atrophy and loss of hair Cool and cyanotic skin Presence of bruit

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2061
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate is indicated as a possible treatment for menopausal disorders, while synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are each identified as potential therapies for alleviating menopausal symptoms. These estrogenic agents represent therapeutic options that can be employed to manage the various clinical manifestations associated with menopause, providing clinicians with multiple pharmacologic choices to address both the broader disorder and its specific symptomatic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate and synthetic conjugated estrogens, A are hormonal agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing estrogen replacement to alleviate symptoms such as hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal atrophy, and other manifestations of estrogen deficiency associated with menopause. Their therapeutic application underscores the importance of estrogen therapy in managing the clinical features of menopausal transition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders. Both belong to the class of synthetic conjugated estrogens and are indicated for alleviating the hormonal deficiencies and associated symptoms of menopause, such as vasomotor instability, urogenital atrophy, and bone loss. Their use underscores the role of estrogen replacement therapy in managing the clinical manifestations of menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1489
retrieved_docs.content: Conjugated estrogens (USP) are a hormone preparation that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, helping to alleviate symptoms such as vasomotor instability, urogenital atrophy, and other manifestations associated with the decline in endogenous estrogen during menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1770
retrieved_docs.content: Estrogeny, a form of estrogen therapy, may be used to treat menopausal disorders, helping to alleviate common symptoms such as hot flashes, vaginal atrophy, and bone loss and thereby improving the overall quality of life for women experiencing menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1489
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia (فقر الدم) is encompassed within the broader category of deficiency and other anemia, indicating that it can arise from nutritional deficiencies as well as other etiologies. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific subtype that falls under the parent condition of anemia, representing a rapid loss of blood leading to reduced hemoglobin levels. Clinically, anemia is closely associated with the symptom of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), a common manifestation that aids in the recognition and management of the disorder. Understanding that anemia, whether due to deficiency, acute hemorrhage, or other causes, frequently presents with weakness is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2749
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate and synthetic conjugated estrogens, B are estrogenic agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing therapeutic options for managing the symptoms associated with menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol and estrogeny are hormonal agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing therapeutic options for alleviating the symptoms associated with the menopausal transition. Both substances are forms of estrogen therapy and are considered in clinical management to improve quality of life for patients experiencing menopausal-related changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol, a potent form of estrogen, may be used to treat menopausal disorders, helping to alleviate symptoms such as hot flashes, vaginal dryness, and bone density loss that commonly occur during the menopausal transition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 365
retrieved_docs.content: Deficiency and other anemia is described as having a broader relationship with فقر الدم, indicating that it encompasses a wide range of anemic conditions. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is identified as a specific subtype that has فقر الدم as its parent category. Clinically, فقر الدم is associated with الشعور بالضعف, reflecting the common presentation of weakness in patients with anemia. The symptom complex malaise and fatigue is mapped to الشعور بالضعف, reinforcing this link, and is also mapped to النوام, suggesting an additional association with nausea or related discomfort. Furthermore, malaise and fatigue is other‑mapped to الضعف العصبي, indicating a connection with neurological weakness. Together, these relationships illustrate that anemia (فقر الدم), including its deficiency‑related and acute posthemorrhagic forms, often manifests with generalized weakness, malaise, fatigue, possible nausea, and neurological weakness, underscoring the importance of recognizing this symptom cluster in patient assessment and care.
